name: JavaScript
description: The language of the web — a high-level, dynamic, interpreted language powering interactive websites, server-side applications, mobile apps, and desktop software.
overview: JavaScript is the most widely-used programming language in the world, running on virtually every web browser and increasingly on servers (Node.js), desktops (Electron), and mobile devices (React Native). Created by Brendan Eich in 1995, JavaScript has evolved from a simple scripting language into a mature, multi-paradigm language with first-class functions, prototypal inheritance, async/await, modules, and a vibrant ecosystem. The ECMAScript specification (ES6/ES2015 through ES2024) brings yearly improvements. JavaScript's single-threaded event loop model, closures, and functional capabilities make it uniquely suited for interactive web applications. With npm's massive package ecosystem (2M+ packages), JavaScript developers can leverage solutions for virtually any problem.

learningPath.title: JavaScript Fundamentals
learningPath.description: Master core JavaScript syntax, data types, operators, control flow, and functions — the building blocks of all JS applications.
topics: History of JavaScript and ECMAScript versions
Setting up development environment (browser console, Node.js, VS Code)
Variables: var, let, const — scope and hoisting differences
Primitive data types: string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, null, symbol
Numbers: integers, floats, NaN, Infinity, arithmetic operators
Strings: literals, template literals, methods (slice, split, replace, includes)
Operators: assignment, comparison (== vs ===), ternary, spread, optional chaining
Loops: for, while, do-while, for-in, for-of
Functions: declaration, expression, arrow functions, parameters
Scope: global, function, block scope and lexical scoping
Basic input/output: alert, prompt, confirm, console.log

learningPath.title: Objects, Arrays & Data Structures
learningPath.description: Deep dive into JavaScript objects, arrays, maps, sets, and modern data structure manipulation.
topics: Objects: literal notation, properties, methods, dynamic keys
Object operations: Object.keys, Object.values, Object.entries, Object.assign
Destructuring: object destructuring and array destructuring
Spread operator (...) with objects and arrays
Arrays: creation, indexing, length, sparse arrays
Array methods: push, pop, shift, unshift, splice, slice, concat, indexOf, includes
Array iteration: forEach, map, filter, reduce, find, some, every, sort
Chaining array methods for data pipelines
Set: creation, add, delete, has, size, iteration
Map: creation, set, get, has, delete, size, iteration vs Object
JSON: JSON.stringify, JSON.parse, serialization best practices
Date: Date object, parsing, formatting, libraries (date-fns)

learningPath.title: Advanced Functions & Closures
learningPath.description: Master JavaScript's function capabilities — closures, higher-order functions, currying, callbacks, and the this keyword.
topics: Functions as first-class citizens (assigned, passed, returned)
this keyword: default binding, implicit binding, explicit binding
call, apply, and bind methods — function borrowing
Arrow functions: lexical this, no arguments, no prototype
Closures: definition, practical use cases (module pattern, factories)
Lexical scoping and the scope chain
Symbol and Symbol.iterator for custom iteration

learningPath.title: Prototypes, Classes & OOP
learningPath.description: Understand JavaScript's prototypal inheritance model, ES6 classes, and object-oriented design patterns.
topics: Constructor functions and the new keyword
ES6 Classes: class declarations, constructor, methods
Class inheritance with extends and super

learningPath.title: Asynchronous JavaScript
learningPath.description: Master asynchronous programming in JavaScript — callbacks, promises, async/await, and the event loop.
topics: The event loop: call stack, task queue, microtask queue
Promises: states (pending, fulfilled, rejected), then, catch, finally
Promise combinators: Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, Promise.race, Promise.any
Error handling with try/catch in async functions
Async iteration: for-await-of and async generators
projects: Async image gallery with lazy loading

learningPath.title: DOM Manipulation & Browser APIs
learningPath.description: Master the Document Object Model, browser APIs, event handling, and building interactive web interfaces.
topics: DOM tree: nodes (element, text, comment, document, documentFragment)
Manipulating: innerHTML, textContent, createElement, appendChild, remove
Attributes and data-* attributes, classList (add, remove, toggle, contains)
Style manipulation: style property, getComputedStyle, CSS classes
Events: addEventListener, event object, event phases (capture, bubble, target)
Custom events with CustomEvent and dispatchEvent
Form handling: input, change, submit, validation, FormData
Intersection Observer for lazy loading and infinite scroll
Resize Observer for element size changes
Geolocation API, Canvas API, Web Audio API
Clipboard API, File API, Blob, URL.createObjectURL

learningPath.title: Modern JavaScript (ES6+ Features)
learningPath.description: Master modern JavaScript features including modules, symbols, proxies, and advanced ES20xx capabilities.
topics: ES6 Modules: export, import, named/default, dynamic import()
Module patterns: revealing, singleton, ES module best practices
Symbol: unique identifiers, well-known symbols (Symbol.iterator, Symbol.toStringTag)
Proxy: traps (get, set, has, deleteProperty, apply, construct)
Reflect: matching proxy traps, method forwarding, meta-operations
TypedArray and ArrayBuffer for binary data
SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics for shared memory
Regular expressions: patterns, flags, exec, match, replace, lookahead
console methods: table, time, group, dir, assert, count
projects: Custom reactive data binding with Proxy

learningPath.title: Testing, Debugging & Tooling
learningPath.description: Learn JavaScript testing frameworks, debugging techniques, build tools, and development workflows.
topics: yarn and pnpm - alternative package managers
Module bundlers: Webpack, Vite, Rollup, esbuild, Parcel
Code formatting: Prettier configuration and integration
Unit testing with Jest (describe, it, expect, mocks, spies)
End-to-end testing with Playwright or Cypress
Debugging: browser DevTools (Sources, Console, Network, Performance)
Source maps for debugging transpiled code
projects: Jest test suite for utility library
E2E test suite for todo app

learningPath.title: Advanced JavaScript & Design Patterns
learningPath.description: Master JavaScript design patterns, performance optimization, security, and advanced runtime concepts.
topics: Design patterns: Module, Observer, Singleton, Factory, Mediator, Facade
Service Worker: caching strategies, offline support, push notifications
Web Components: Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, HTML Templates
Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) and content security policy (CSP)
Memory leaks in JS: closures, detached DOM, circular references
Garbage collection in V8: generational, mark-sweep, Mark-Compact
Performance optimization: reflow/repaint, debounce, throttle, requestAnimationFrame
Secure JavaScript: XSS prevention, CSRF tokens, input sanitization
Workers: Web Workers, Service Workers, Worklets
Atomics and SharedArrayBuffer for worker communication

learningPath.title: JavaScript Ecosystem & Production
learningPath.description: Navigate the modern JavaScript ecosystem — frameworks, state management, deployment, and career-ready skills.
topics: React fundamentals: components, JSX, hooks, props, state
Vue.js: reactive data, directives, components, Composition API
Angular: TypeScript, modules, components, services, DI
State management: Redux, Zustand, Pinia, MobX, Jotai
Client-side routing: React Router, Vue Router, TanStack Router
Static Site Generation: Astro, Eleventy, Hugo
Styling approaches: CSS Modules, Tailwind CSS, CSS-in-JS (styled-components)
REST API clients: axios, ky, react-query, SWR
Deployment: Netlify, Vercel, Cloudflare Pages, AWS S3+CloudFront
Code splitting, lazy loading, bundle optimization
projects: Full-stack CRUD app with React + Node
Next.js blog with SSR and SSG

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the difference between var, let, and const.
interviewQuestions.answer: var: function-scoped, hoisted (initialized as undefined), can be redeclared and reassigned. let: block-scoped, hoisted but not initialized (Temporal Dead Zone), can be reassigned but not redeclared in same scope. const: block-scoped, hoisted but not initialized, must be initialized at declaration, cannot be reassigned (but object properties can be mutated). Use const by default, let when reassignment is needed, never var in modern JS.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the event loop and how does it work?
interviewQuestions.answer: The event loop is JavaScript's concurrency model. It continuously checks if the call stack is empty. When empty, it processes tasks from the task queue (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O) and microtask queue (Promise.then, catch, finally, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver). Microtasks have priority over tasks. The event loop enables non-blocking async operations despite JavaScript being single-threaded.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain closures with a practical example.
interviewQuestions.answer: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its outer (enclosing) scope even after the outer function has returned. Example: function makeCounter() { let count = 0; return function() { return ++count; }; } const counter = makeCounter(); counter() // 1, counter() // 2. Uses: data privacy, module pattern, event handlers, memoization, factory functions.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
interviewQuestions.answer: == (abstract equality) compares after type coercion, applying complex conversion rules. === (strict equality) compares without type coercion — returns false if types differ. Always use === to avoid unexpected coercion bugs. Exception: x == null safely checks both null and undefined. Key coercion: '' == 0 is true, false == 'false' is false, null == undefined is true.

interviewQuestions.question: What is prototypal inheritance?
interviewQuestions.answer: JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance where objects inherit directly from other objects via the prototype chain. Each object has an internal [[Prototype]] (accessible via __proto__ or Object.getPrototypeOf). When accessing a property, JS traverses the chain until found. Object.create() creates objects with a specified prototype. ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over prototypal inheritance with constructor functions.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the concept of Hoisting.
interviewQuestions.answer: Hoisting is JS's behavior of moving declarations to the top of their scope during compilation. var declarations are hoisted and initialized to undefined. let/const declarations are hoisted but not initialized (Temporal Dead Zone — ReferenceError if accessed before declaration). Function declarations are fully hoisted (definition available). Function expressions (var/let/const) follow their variable hoisting rules.

interviewQuestions.question: How does the this keyword work in JavaScript?
interviewQuestions.answer: this depends on execution context: global context — window (browser) / global (Node); function — window (strict: undefined); method — the calling object; arrow function — lexical this (from enclosing scope); constructor (new) — the new instance; event handler — the element; explicit: call, apply, bind set this manually; class methods — the class instance. Understanding this is critical for avoiding common bugs.

interviewQuestions.question: What are Promises and how do they differ from callbacks?
interviewQuestions.answer: Promises represent eventual completion (or failure) of an async operation. States: pending, fulfilled, rejected. Benefits over callbacks: chaining instead of nesting (avoid callback hell), unified error handling (.catch()), composition (Promise.all, Promise.race), built-in cancellation with AbortController. A Promise can only resolve/reject once and maintains its state, unlike callbacks which can be called multiple times.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between null and undefined?
interviewQuestions.answer: undefined means a variable has been declared but not assigned a value (default). null is an intentional absence of any object value — must be explicitly assigned. typeof undefined is 'undefined', typeof null is 'object' (historical bug). null == undefined is true (== coercion), null === undefined is false. Function parameters default to undefined. JSON doesn't support undefined but supports null.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain debouncing and throttling.
interviewQuestions.answer: Debouncing delays execution until after a specified quiet period — useful for search input (wait until user stops typing). Throttling limits execution to at most once per specified interval — useful for scroll/resize handlers. Both improve performance for frequent events. Debounce: clearTimeout on each call, setTimeout on last. Throttle: use timestamp or flag to track last execution.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between LocalStorage, SessionStorage, and Cookies?
interviewQuestions.answer: LocalStorage: persists until explicitly cleared, ~5MB, synchronous, not sent to server. SessionStorage: persists only for the current tab/window session, cleared on close, ~5MB. Cookies: sent with every HTTP request, max 4KB, can set expiry, supports HttpOnly/Secure flags. For client-only data use localStorage/sessionStorage. For auth tokens use httpOnly cookies. IndexedDB is used for larger structured data.

interviewQuestions.question: How does async/await work under the hood?
interviewQuestions.answer: Async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises + generators. An async function returns a Promise. await pauses execution of the async function, yielding control back to the event loop, and resumes when the awaited Promise resolves (or throws on rejection). The compiler transforms async functions into state machines using generators (or directly into Promises). Each await is a suspension point.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the Module pattern and its benefits.
interviewQuestions.answer: The Module pattern uses closures to create private scopes and expose a public API. Benefits: encapsulation (private variables/methods), namespace isolation (avoids global pollution), dependency management, maintainability. Modern JS uses ES modules (import/export) natively. IIFE-based modules created the pattern before native module support. Revealing Module pattern assigns public references to private functions.

interviewQuestions.question: What are Web Workers and when would you use them?
interviewQuestions.answer: Web Workers run JavaScript in background threads separate from the main execution thread. They communicate via postMessage and onmessage. Use cases: CPU-intensive calculations, image/video processing, large data parsing, compression, and tasks that shouldn't block UI. Limitations: no DOM access, no window/document, limited APIs. SharedWorkers can be shared across multiple tabs. Service Workers are a special type for network proxying.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you optimize JavaScript performance?
interviewQuestions.answer: Techniques: minimize DOM access (batch reads/writes, virtual DOM), use event delegation instead of many handlers, debounce/throttle frequent events, use requestAnimationFrame for visual updates, avoid layout thrashing, code-split with dynamic imports, lazy load images/components, use Web Workers for heavy computation, avoid memory leaks (clean up listeners/timeouts), use performance.now() and Chrome DevTools Performance tab.

books.title: Eloquent JavaScript
books.author: Marijn Haverbeke
books.description: A modern introduction to programming with JavaScript covering fundamentals, browser APIs, Node.js, and building real applications.

books.title: You Don't Know JS (Book Series)
books.author: Kyle Simpson
books.description: Deep dive into JavaScript's core mechanisms — scope, closures, this, prototypes, types, async, and ES6. Six-book series.

books.title: JavaScript: The Good Parts
books.author: Douglas Crockford
books.description: Classic guide to JavaScript's best features covering functions, objects, inheritance, arrays, regex, and style.

books.title: JavaScript: The Definitive Guide
books.author: David Flanagan
books.description: Comprehensive reference covering core language, client-side JavaScript, Node.js, and all modern ES features.

books.title: Clean Code in JavaScript
books.author: James Padolsey
books.description: Write robust, maintainable JavaScript code following clean code principles, SOLID, and testing best practices.

books.title: Learning JavaScript Design Patterns
books.author: Addy Osmani
books.description: Explore JavaScript design patterns including creational, structural, behavioral, MV* patterns, and modern React patterns.

books.title: Testing JavaScript Applications
books.author: Lucas da Costa
books.description: Complete guide to testing JavaScript from unit tests to E2E, covering Jest, Cypress, Playwright, and testing strategies.

books.title: High Performance JavaScript
books.author: Nicholas C. Zakas
books.description: Performance optimization for JavaScript covering loading, DOM, algorithms, deployment, and profiling.

books.title: JavaScript Cookbook
books.author: Adam D. Scott & Shelley Powers
books.description: Practical solutions for everyday JavaScript problems covering browsers, Node, data, and frameworks.

books.title: Functional-Light JavaScript
books.author: Kyle Simpson
books.description: Balanced approach to functional programming in JavaScript covering pure functions, composition, immutability, and recursion.

projects.title: Interactive To-Do App with Drag & Drop
projects.description: Full-featured to-do app with CRUD, categories, localStorage persistence, drag-drop reordering, and dark mode toggle.

projects.title: Weather Dashboard with Fetch API
projects.description: Display real-time weather from OpenWeather API with search, 5-day forecast, charts, and geolocation.

projects.title: Real-Time Collaborative Whiteboard
projects.description: Canvas-based whiteboard with drawing tools, real-time sync via WebSockets, undo/redo, and export to image.

projects.title: Custom JavaScript Framework (Mini React)
projects.description: Build a minimal reactive UI library with virtual DOM, JSX-like syntax, component model, hooks, and state management.

projects.title: Browser-Based Code Editor
projects.description: Monaco/CodeMirror-based code editor with syntax highlighting, linting, auto-complete, file tree, and local execution.

projects.title: PWA E-Commerce App
projects.description: Progressive web app with offline support, service worker caching, push notifications, add-to-homescreen, and product catalog.

projects.title: API Rate Limiter Library
projects.description: JavaScript library implementing token bucket and sliding window rate limiting with configurable limits, queue, and retry logic.

projects.title: ESLint Plugin for Custom Rules
projects.description: Create an ESLint plugin with custom AST-based rules for enforcing project-specific code conventions.

bestPractices: Use const by default, let when reassignment is needed — never use var in modern code
Use strict equality (===) and strict inequality (!==) instead of == and !=
Prefer arrow functions for callbacks and when preserving lexical this, use named functions for standalone definitions
Use template literals instead of string concatenation
Use object and array destructuring for cleaner code
Use default parameters instead of manual undefined checks
Use optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) for safer property access
Keep functions small and focused on single responsibility
Use early returns to reduce nesting
Prefer map/filter/reduce over for loops for array transformations
Use async/await over raw Promise chains for readability
Handle promise rejections with .catch() or try/catch in async functions
Avoid mutation — create new arrays/objects with spread or methods
Use named exports over default exports for better refactoring
Organize code by feature (not by type) in larger codebases
Write self-documenting code — use meaningful variable and function names
Use JSDoc comments for public API documentation
Use ESLint and Prettier to enforce consistent code style
Write tests (Jest, Testing Library) for critical business logic
Use TypeScript for any project with more than a few files

careerOpportunities.description: Build and maintain web interfaces using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and modern frameworks under senior guidance.
Develop interactive user interfaces, optimize performance, implement designs, and integrate with backend APIs.
Build end-to-end features across frontend and backend using Node.js and modern JS frameworks.
Specialize in building SPAs with React, managing state, optimizing renders, and implementing complex UI patterns.
Design and build server-side APIs, microservices, and backend systems using Node.js and Express/Fastify.
Develop and maintain build tools, CLI tools, bundlers, linters, and developer tooling using JavaScript.
Build cross-platform mobile applications using React Native, bridging native modules, and publishing to app stores.
Lead frontend teams, drive technical decisions, review architecture, and guide career development.
